title: Your Attention Is the Bottleneck, Not Your Agents — Zack Proser, WorkOS
summary: Zack Proser argues that scaling parallel AI agents for development work does not solve developer burnout because human attention remains the hard constraint. He proposes a four-layer architecture including signal agents to reduce context switching, verification gates for safety, weekly introspection loops on conversation logs, and biometric feedback integration to make agent-assisted workflows sustainable.
writeup: WorkOS applied AI engineer Zach argues that agent capability has outpaced human cognitive capacity: the real bottleneck in AI-assisted development is now attention, not tooling. He grounds the talk in a concrete example of using Claude Code with MCP-connected Slack so an agent could write, deploy, test, and verify a bug fix in a closed loop without the developer re-entering context. The through-line is a practical framework for staying productive without burning out: signal layers (letting agents filter noise), voice-first input (sustaining 184 wpm across parallel sessions), and remote-control sessions that let diffuse-mode thinking produce work while away from the desk.
key_points: Human attention is now the hard constraint on AI-assisted development, not agent throughput: Simon Willison noted that running four parallel agents leaves him wiped out by 11:00 a.m., illustrating that linear scaling of workload with agent count accelerates burnout rather than capacity.

Giving Claude Code MCP access to Slack allowed an agent to autonomously post a test message to the blog-post channel, observe the bot's response, verify the acronym-safe sentence-case fix was correct, and close the loop without the developer returning to check intermediate state.

Voice-first input at roughly 184 words per minute enables parallel orchestration across multiple Cursor windows, Codex, and Claude tabs simultaneously, putting agents in motion before a keyboard-first developer has finished typing the first prompt.

Claude Code's remote-control flag lets a running desktop session be monitored and steered from a phone on a different network, making diffuse-mode thinking (shower principle) actionable: insights that arrive away from the desk can now be fed directly to an agent still executing.

Agents can loop indefinitely toward a verification criterion, but human judgment about whether that criterion actually maps to business or product needs remains irreplaceable, forming the basis for the human-agent division of labor the speaker describes.

Routing Slack at-mentions and DMs through an agent filter on a loop, with Linear ticket deduplication via MCP, acts as a signal layer that preserves developer focus by blocking the context-switching that degrades attention under high-traffic conditions.
